rulings.Case Title: FORD MOTOR CREDIT COMPANY LLC VS. SAIRA I FREGOSO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Claim Of Exemption
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, November 21, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF FORD MOTOR CREDIT COMPANY LLC'S Notice Of Hearing On Claim Of Exemption. Hearing required. Defendant shall bring the following evidence to the hearing to substantiate her claim that the money in the levied upon bank account belonged to her roommates: (1) evidence of the transfers of funds from her roommates to Defendant and (2) checks (or other proof of payment) from the previous two months showing the amount of rent paid to the landlord. If the rent is not divided equally among the roommates, Defendant is also required to bring evidence that establishes the amount of rent each roommate pays. Additionally, to the extent that Defendant claims the other money levied upon is exempt, Defendant must bring proof that the funds can be traced to her paid earnings. (See CCP sec. 704.070.) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: NANTONG JOYFUL FASHION CO., LTD et al VS. GEORGIOU STUDIO, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Summ Adj; Evidence
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, November 21, 2014, Line 13, PLAINTIFFS NANTONG JOYFUL FASHION CO., LTD, BEIJING MENGYAZI GARMENTS CO., LTD, BEIJING YANDI GARMENTS CO., LTD'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Summary Adjudication. The Motion is granted in part and denied in part. Summary adjudication is granted in favor of Plaintiff Nantong Joyful Fashion ("NJF") on its cause of action for breach of contract. Plaintiff met its burden of showing that Defendant agreed to the terms of the "Sales Contract," and that Defendant failed to pay the amount owed. Defendant failed to rebut that showing because evidence of a prior agreement between the parties does not negate the terms agreed to by the parties in the sales contract. Summary adjudication is denied for Plaintiffs Beijing Mengyazi Garments Co., Ltd. and Beijing Yandi Garments Co., Ltd. (collectively "BMYG") for breach of contract. BMYG's evidence indicates that the parties agreed to payment on a consignment basis, yet BMYG failed to produce any evidence that all of the merchandise received by Defendants was actually sold. Absent this evidence, BMYG has failed to establish what amount was due from Defendants pursuant to the agreement. Summary adjudication is denied as moot to NJF for its cause of action for common counts as NJF has shown that it is entitled to judgment pursuant to breach of express contract. Judgment is denied as to BMYG because BMYG has failed to establish that no triable issues exist as to the amount due under the contract. For this same lack of evidence, judgment is also denied for conversion. Judgment is granted in favor of Plaintiffs as to all of Defendants' affirmative defenses, as none of these defenses are available to Defendants. (See UMF Issue 8, #2; UMF Issue 9, #1.) Judgment is granted in favor of NJF as to all causes of action in the Cross-Complaint. The evidence indicates that the parties entered a purchase and sale agreement and Defendants have presented no evidence raising a triable issue as to any causes of action against NJF. Judgment is denied as to BMYG on all causes of action in the Cross-Complaint because triable issues exist as to whether the parties were operating as a partnership or joint venture. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: AMY WELS VS. STARBUCKS CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, November 21, 2014, Line 6, DEFENDANTS STARBUCKS CORPORATION'S Motion For Summary Judgment. The motion for summary judgment is denied. On June 7, 2013, plaintiff was injured when a third party patron ("patron") flew into a rage and threw a display that landed on plaintiff. A store owner owes a duty to undertake reasonable and appropriate measures to protect patrons from imminent criminal conduct. See Delgado v. Trax Bar & Grill (2005) 36 Cal.4th 224, 241. The court finds that there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether defendant breached this duty of care by failing to ban the patron from the premises or by lying to him and refusing to process his gift card request. The evidence indicates that defendant had numerous prior encounters with the patron and defendant should have known that he posed a threat. A month prior to the incident that forms the basis of plaintiff's complaint, defendant's employee Kaira Etrata asked the patron to move because he was blocking the entrance to the premises. The patron refused to move and rudely called her "[a] five-cent ho." (Etrata Depo., 44:4-47:5). Two to three weeks prior to the incident, the patron created a mess at the premises, spoke loudly, and told defendant that "you can't tell me what to do." (Shaver Dec., par. 3; Shaver Depo., 17:19-18:9). Lastly, on the day of the incident, defendant provoked the patron by lying to him and failing to process his gift card request. (Ertata Depo., 75:21-77:16). A reasonable trier of fact could infer that defendant knew or should have known that the patron was aggressive and volatile and posed a risk to other customers at the coffee bar. The court further notes that defendant's failure to train its employees, including the shift manager, on how to deal with a disruptive customer exacerbated the situation. (Erata Depo., 33:5-9; 39:22-40:1). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: VALLEY CENTER EQUITY HOLDINGS, LLC, A DELAWARE et al VS. GCL-SOL ORCHARD HOLDINGS, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Gcl-Sol Orchard Holdings, Llcs Notice Of Motion To Enforce And Compel Production Of Business Records From Non-Party North American Development Bank Pursuant To An Amended Subpoena
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, November 21, 2014, Line 2, DEFENDANT GCL-SOL ORCHARD HOLDINGS, LLC, Motion To Enforce And Compel Production Of Business Records From Non-Party North American Development Bank Pursuant To An Amended Subpoena. Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =(302/JPT)
